iocs.capabilities: Keylogging, screen capture, clipboard data collection
iocs.comment: SlimAgent keylogger
BeardShell trojan
BeardShell C2 infrastructure, legit service abused by the malware
Covenant C2 infrastructure, legit service abused by the malware
BeardShell only executes in taskhost.exe or taskhostw.exe
SlimAgent only executes in explorer.exe

ttps.comment: Acquire Infrastructure: Web Services - BeardShell relies on Icedrive cloud storage. Covenant relies on Filen cloud storage.
Develop Capabilities: Malware - BeardShell and SlimAgent are custom malware.
Command and Scripting Interpreter: PowerShell - BeardShell executes PowerShell commands.
Shared Modules - BeardShell and SlimAgent are full-fledged DLL files.
Event Triggered Execution: Component Object Model Hijacking - BeardShell and SlimAgent are made persistent by hijacking COM objects.
Obfuscated Files or Information - BeardShell Icedrive token decryption is obfuscated.
Deobfuscate/Decode Files or Information - BeardShell decrypts its strings.
Execution Guardrails - BeardShell only executes in taskhost.exe or taskhostw.exe. SlimAgent only executes in explorer.exe.
Hide Artifacts - SlimAgent logs are written into a hidden file.
System Information Discovery - BeardShell sends a fingerprint of the compromised machine.
Data from Local System - BeardShell, Covenant, and SlimAgent collect data from a compromised machine.
Input Capture: Keylogging - SlimAgent performs keylogging.
Screen Capture - SlimAgent captures screenshots of the compromised machine.
Clipboard Data - SlimAgent collects clipboard data.
Data Obfuscation - BeardShell exfiltrates data in fake images.
Application Layer Protocol: Web Protocols - BeardShell and Covenant use HTTPS for C&C.
Web Service - BeardShell gets commands from Icedrive. Covenant gets commands from Filen.
Encrypted Channel: Asymmetric Cryptography - BeardShell communications with Icedrive are encrypted using HTTPS. Covenant communications with its controller uses RSA-encrypted session keys.
Exfiltration Over Web Service - BeardShell exfiltrates data to Icedrive. Covenant exfiltrates data to Filen.

queries.comment: SlimAgent and BeardShell execution, dll injection
SlimAgent 2024 file detection by specific function names. content:"FN_log_window_text"
Modified Covenant file detection by specific function names. content:"GruntUUID" and content:"filenClient"

xrefs.comment: BeardShell only executes in taskhost.exe or taskhostw.exe
SlimAgent only executes in explorer.exe
BeardShell relies on Icedrive cloud storage
Custom malware capability is delivered through DLL modules.
Module execution is followed by COM hijacking persistence.
Persistent components execute scripted commands.
Execution path enforces host guardrails before activity.
Guardrail checks are followed by host fingerprinting.
System discovery guides local data collection.
Collected data is packaged with obfuscation.
Obfuscated data is sent over encrypted channels.
Encrypted communication is carried over HTTPS.
C2 traffic uses cloud web services.
Web service channel is used for exfiltration.

iocs.comment: C2 server for additional payload retrieval
Commercial VPN segment used for egress to Israel targets
Israeli VPN node used for egress
Zero-trust Wireguard-based mesh network deployed for internal lateral movement
Disk encryption utility downloaded for destructive encryption
LSASS credential dumping pre-wiping. Value is keywords enumeration, cmdline is <path>rundll32.exe <paht>comsvcs.dll, MiniDump <PID> <path_filename>.dmp full
Copies registry hives from VSS (SYSTEM hive). Value is cmdline start, cmdline is wmic[.exe] process call create "reg.exe save HKLM\SYSTEM <path_filename>"

iocs.name: handala.exe
iocs.comment: Handala Wiper, dropped by initial access vector and used for destructive payload execution

ttps.comment: Exploit Public-Facing Application - Compromised VPN accounts for initial access.
External Remote Services - RDP for hands-on lateral movement.
Lateral Tool Transfer - NetBird deployment via browser download.
OS Credential Dumping: LSASS Memory - comsvcs.dll MiniDump.
Account Discovery: Domain Accounts - ADRecon PowerShell.
Data Destruction - Handala Wiper (MBR/file overwrite).
Disk Wipe: Disk Structure Wipe - Custom MBR wiping.
Inhibit System Recovery - Multiple parallel wiping methods.
Data Encrypted for Impact - VeraCrypt disk encryption.
Command and Scripting Interpreter: PowerShell - AI-assisted wiping script.
Execution Guardrails - Domain Admin creds validation pre-impact.
Valid Accounts - Compromised DA credentials for GPO deployment.

queries.value: Process | where ProcessImageName in~('rundll32.exe', 'powershell.exe') | where ProcessCommandLine contains 'comsvcs.dll' and ProcessCommandLine contains 'MiniDump'
Process | where ProcessImageName in~('wmic.exe', 'powershell.exe') | where ProcessCommandLine contains 'process call create' and ProcessCommandLine contains 'reg.exe save'
DeviceNetworkEvents | where RemoteIP in ('107.189.19.52', '169.150.227.0/24', '146.185.219.235') | where ActionType == 'ConnectionSuccess'
queries.comment: Suspicious C2/VPN connections from Iranian actors. Here could also detect IR IPs

xrefs.comment: Initial remote access is followed by tooling transfer.
Transferred tooling supports credential dumping.
Credential access enables AD account discovery.
Discovery helps operationalize valid domain accounts.
Validated privileged access is checked before impact.
Guardrails precede scripted destructive orchestration.
PowerShell execution drives destructive actions.
General destruction escalates to disk structure wipe.
Destructive phase includes inhibiting recovery.
Destruction is combined with encryption for impact.

iocs.comment: Dropper DLL (Variant 1) filename: 2_2.d
Dropper DLL (Variant 1) filename: 2_2.d
Dropper DLL (Variant 1) filename: 2_2.d
PixyNetLoader dropper DLL (Variant 2) filename: table.d
Shellcode loader filename: EhStoreShell.dll
PNG container with embedded shellcode filename: SplashScreen.png
Malicious domain used to host payloads
Malicious domain used in campaign infrastructure
URL hosting MiniDoor dropper DLL
URL hosting PixyNetLoader
EhStoreShell.dll executes malicious logic only when loaded by explorer.exe
Scheduled task creation command used for staged execution
Attacker-controlled mailbox used by the Outlook VBA forwarding workflow

ttps.comment: Phishing: Spearphishing Attachment - weaponized RTFs delivered as attachments.
Exploitation for Client Execution - CVE-2026-21509 exploited by crafted RTF files.
Scheduled Task/Job: Scheduled Task - OneDriveHealth task created via schtasks.exe.
Event Triggered Execution: Component Object Model Hijacking - persistence via CLSID InProcServer32 hijack.
Office Application Startup: Add-ins - malicious Outlook VBA project loaded at startup.
Obfuscated Files or Information: Steganography - shellcode hidden in SplashScreen.png LSB data.
Virtualization/Sandbox Evasion: Time Based Checks - Sleep timing guardrail check in loader.
Email Collection - MiniDoor Outlook VBA forwards mailbox messages to attacker-controlled addresses.
Application Layer Protocol: Web Protocols - Covenant Grunt uses HTTPS for C2 traffic.
Web Service: Bidirectional Communication - Filen API abused as C2Bridge channel.
Execution Guardrails: Mutual Exclusion - campaign components create mutexes to avoid duplicate execution.

vulns.comment: Crafted RTF exploit used in Operation Neusploit delivery chain.

queries.value: DeviceProcessEvents | where FileName =~ 'schtasks.exe' | where ProcessCommandLine has '/Create' and ProcessCommandLine has 'OneDriveHealth' and ProcessCommandLine has 'office.xml'
queries.comment: Detect scheduled task creation observed in PixyNetLoader chain
Detect network activity to campaign infrastructure

queries.value: rule APT28_Operation_Neusploit_PixyNetLoader { strings: $m1 = "asagdugughi41" ascii $m2 = "OneDriveHealth" ascii $m3 = "EhStoreShell.dll" ascii condition: 2 of ($m*) }
queries.comment: Static detections based on strings explicitly documented in the report

xrefs.comment: Operation Neusploit chain: exploit RTF -> dropper DLL -> persistence via COM hijack and scheduled task -> shellcode loader -> Covenant Grunt C2
Spearphishing attachment triggers exploit execution.
Variant 1 establishes Outlook add-in startup behavior.
Outlook add-in persistence enables mailbox collection.
Variant 2 sets scheduled task based staged execution.
Scheduled execution supports COM hijacking persistence.
Persistent loader retrieves steganographic payload content.
Stego payload execution includes sandbox timing checks.
Evasion path chains into mutual exclusion guardrails.
Guardrail-validated implant proceeds to HTTPS C2.
HTTPS C2 is implemented over bidirectional web service.

ttps.comment: Adversary-in-the-Middle behavior on gateway/edge devices is central to DKnife operations.
Framework performs deep packet inspection and traffic monitoring on routed traffic.
C2 and telemetry channels operate over web protocols (HTTP/HTTPS).
Collected credentials and traffic-derived data are exfiltrated to remote C2 endpoints.
Update and binary responses are manipulated to deliver attacker-controlled payloads.

C2 infrastructure and protocol endpoints support web-protocol C2 and exfiltration.
Redirected update package delivery URL is aligned with response manipulation behavior.
Traffic interception enables controlled C2 redirection over web protocols.
C2 communication flow precedes data exfiltration to remote infrastructure.

StoatWaffle IOC listed by NTT Security Japan
StoatWaffle IOC listed by NTT Security Japan
StoatWaffle IOC listed by NTT Security Japan
StoatWaffle IOC listed by NTT Security Japan
Malicious VS Code task configuration executed on folder open
Batch downloader launched from the initial task chain
First-stage StoatWaffle Node.js downloader
Downloaded Vercel-hosted payloads are executed through cmd.exe
First-stage downloader polling path on StoatWaffle C2
Second-stage downloader polling path on StoatWaffle C2
Credential theft upload path
Installed software inventory upload path
RAT command retrieval path
RAT command result upload path

ttps.comment: User opens and trusts a malicious VS Code repository, triggering task execution.
Initial payloads are executed via cmd.exe on Windows.
StoatWaffle modules are implemented in and execute Node.js JavaScript code.
Multiple downloader stages retrieve additional payloads and modules from attacker infrastructure.
Stealer module extracts credentials and extension data from web browsers.
Stealer module enumerates installed software and sends the inventory to C2.
Stolen data and command results are exfiltrated over the same C2 channel.
Loader, stealer, and RAT modules communicate with HTTP-based C2 endpoints.

queries.comment: Detect outbound connections to StoatWaffle IOC IP addresses

queries.value: DeviceProcessEvents | where FileName in~ ('cmd.exe','node.exe') | where ProcessCommandLine has_any ('vscode-bootstrap.cmd','env.npl')
queries.comment: Detect VS Code / Node.js execution chain associated with StoatWaffle

xrefs.comment: Malicious tasks.json triggers the staged downloader chain.
The VS Code, command shell, and Node.js stages map to user execution and staged payload retrieval.
HTTP API paths support C2 polling, command execution, and result exfiltration.
Upload paths are used for credential theft output, software discovery output, and exfiltration.
Repository opening and trust lead to staged downloader retrieval.
Downloaded modules proceed to browser credential theft.
Collected credentials are then exfiltrated to the C2.
